Q1: I've always been someone who learns best by listening, so I built my study routine around that. I'd dedicate about 16 hours a week, usually in blocks where I could listen to lectures and then talk through concepts out loud to myself. Being 27 and having been through some tough times academically before, I've learned that consistency matters more than cramming. I tried to keep mornings for the heavy cognitive work and evenings for review, though some weeks were harder than others emotionally.
Q2: I aimed for regularity because structure helps me manage my anxiety, but honestly, life doesn't always cooperate. When deadlines approached, I'd shift things around and sometimes sacrifice sleep, which I know isn't healthy. My extracurricular commitments also meant some weeks I had to compress my study time. The pattern was mostly consistent, but there were definitely fluctuations when stress peaked or when I was feeling particularly low.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew new lecture content was posted or when I needed to check on discussion threads. I also made it a habit to check in before starting any study session to see if there were announcements or feedback on my work. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel connected to the course, especially on days when I was feeling isolated or sad. The peer support group we had going also motivated me to stay active in the discussions.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging into the LMS became almost automatic for me. I'd do it first thing in the morning with my coffee, just to see what was happening. It became part of my daily rhythm, like checking email. Having that routine helped ground me, especially during periods when my anxiety was spiking and I needed something predictable to hold onto.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I'd watch them multiple times, sometimes at different speeds, really letting the explanations sink in. The discussion forums were also valuable because engaging with peers helped me process ideas verbally, even if it was through text. When the peer support intervention started, those interactions became a lifeline for me academically and emotionally.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't engage much with some of the supplementary reading PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but rarely got through them because reading dense text isn't how I learn best. Some of the optional quizzes I also skipped when time was tight or when I was feeling overwhelmed. It wasn't that I didn't value them; I just had to prioritize based on my energy levels and what would actually stick.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to the bigger picture or to assessment requirements. If I could listen to something and feel like I genuinely understood a concept better afterward, that was gold. I also valued activities that allowed for some interaction or discussion because that social element helped combat the isolation I sometimes felt. Basically, if it engaged my ears and my mind together, I was in.
Q8: Optional activities always carried this weight of guilt for me. I knew they could help, but when anxiety was high and time was limited, they felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required activities had clear stakes, so I'd push through even when I was struggling. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, and sometimes later never came. The distinction definitely affected how I prioritized my limited energy.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of audio files and would listen to them on walks or during my commute. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos on related topics to supplement my understanding. Sometimes I'd record myself summarizing concepts and play those back. A lot of my best learning happened away from my computer, just me and my headphones working through ideas out loud.
Q10: There were many times I'd be pacing around my apartment, talking through exam concepts to myself for hours, and none of that would show up anywhere. I also had study sessions with a couple of peers from the support group where we'd meet in person and discuss material verbally. Those conversations were incredibly valuable for my learning, but the LMS would just show I hadn't logged in that day.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture recordings and then listen to them repeatedly offline over several days. Once I had the files, I didn't need to go back to the LMS for that content. This was especially true during weeks when my internet was being used heavily for other things or when I just wanted to study without the distraction of being online.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, usually mid-semester, when everything felt heavier. My anxiety would spike around major deadlines, and there were days when just opening my laptop felt like too much. The sadness would creep in, making it hard to care about coursework. During those times, the peer support really mattered—having people check in on me kept me from completely withdrawing.
Q13: Fatigue was probably my biggest enemy. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars sometimes meant I had to make hard choices about what got my attention. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those modules temporarily, which probably showed up as gaps in my engagement data.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have a video playing but my mind would be elsewhere, caught up in anxious thoughts about whether I was doing enough or worried about things outside of school. I'd technically be 'watching' content but absorbing almost nothing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed writing assignments more than anything else. Even though I knew they were important and my scores were decent, the act of writing triggered a lot of anxiety for me. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, which was sometimes true but often just an excuse. The peer support group actually helped me break this pattern by creating accountability.
Q16: Deadlines were like a double-edged sword for me. They provided necessary structure and forced me to engage, but they also amplified my anxiety significantly. I'd often see spikes in my LMS activity in the days before something was due, frantically reviewing materials. The pressure helped me perform, but it also took a toll on my wellbeing. I've learned to start earlier, but old habits die hard.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me. When I received positive feedback, it boosted my motivation and made me want to engage more with the course. Constructive criticism sometimes sent me into a spiral of self-doubt, but I've built enough resilience to eventually use it productively. I'd often revisit feedback multiple times, listening to any audio comments repeatedly to make sure I understood what I needed to improve.
Q18: The course structure was generally helpful because it gave me a roadmap to follow. Having clear modules and deadlines meant I could plan my 16 hours of study time effectively. However, when multiple things were due in the same week, the structure felt suffocating rather than supportive. The peer support component was a structural element that really enhanced my experience and kept me connected.
Q19: The availability of audio and video content made engagement much easier for someone like me who learns by listening. What made it harder was when important information was only available in dense text documents. Also, the discussion forum interface wasn't the most intuitive, which sometimes discouraged me from posting even when I had something to contribute. Clearer navigation would have helped during high-stress periods.
Q20: I think that could reflect someone who's struggling but trying hard to find their footing. They might be searching for understanding, watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, but not feeling confident enough to submit work. It could also be anxiety-driven perfectionism—engaging with everything but being paralyzed when it comes to producing something that will be judged. I've been close to that pattern myself.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience to some degree. My exam score was quite high, but my LMS engagement was only moderate because so much of my learning happened offline. Someone might download materials once and study them extensively away from the platform. Or they might have strong foundational knowledge and not need to engage as heavily with every resource. The LMS doesn't capture everything.
Q22: I think course logs can accurately show patterns of access and timing—when someone is active, how often they're checking in, what they're clicking on. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures on walks, or the depth of conversations in peer study sessions. They show behavior, not learning.
Q23: For me, being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just passively consuming it. It's about feeling connected to the content and to other people in the course. It's showing up even when you're struggling, asking for help when you need it, and caring about your own growth. True engagement is as much emotional and social as it is intellectual.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the peer support intervention meant to me, or how many times I almost gave up but didn't. It wouldn't capture the sadness I carried through much of the semester or the anxiety that made every login feel like a small victory. It can't measure resilience or the effort it takes to keep going when your motivation is low. My distinction grade came from a lot of invisible struggle.
